title: Lamb dhansak
subtitle: Parsi lamb, lentil, and vegetable curry
excerpt: A Parsi dish of slow-cooked lamb in a thick, tangy sauce of red lentils, butternut squash, tomatoes, and tamarind, seasoned with a spice blend that is hot, sweet, and sour at once. One of the most complex curries in the British-Indian repertoire. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Dhansak is a signature dish of the Parsi community of Mumbai (Bombay), developed from a fusion of Persian and Gujarati cooking. It is served at Parsi celebrations and mourning meals. Documented by Niloufer Ichaporia King in My Bombay Kitchen (2007) and adapted for the British-Indian restaurant context by Pat Chapman in The Curry Club Indian Restaurant Cookbook (1984).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes very well for up to three months. The lentils continue to thicken on freezing; add a splash of water when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Improves markedly on the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead — the lentils absorb the lamb flavour overnight and the dish tastes noticeably richer.

content.text: Dhansak is a layered dish — the lentils and vegetables cook down to a thick, almost smooth base, and the lamb braises in that base until falling apart. The tamarind and the sweetness of the squash create the characteristic sour-sweet quality. It takes 90 minutes but requires very little active attention; the oven or the lowest hob setting does the work.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into 3 cm cubes
or the juice of 1 lemon if unavailable

content.text: Melt {{ghee}} of ghee in a large heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the lamb cubes in batches for 3–4 minutes per batch until well coloured on all sides. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{onion}} finely chopped onions to the same pan and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{ginger-root}} of ginger; cook for 2 minutes. Add {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{turmeric}} of ground turmeric, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, and {{garam-masala}} of garam masala; stir for 1 minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes and stir well. Return the lamb to the pan. Add {{red-lentils}} of rinsed lentils and {{butternut-squash}} of butternut squash. Pour over enough water to just cover (about 600 ml). Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a very low simmer. Cover and cook for 60 minutes until the lamb is tender and the lentils have dissolved into the sauce. Stir in {{tamarind-paste}} of tamarind paste. If the sauce is very thick, add a splash of water. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with basmati rice and scatter with coriander.

items.cause: Simmered too quickly so the lentils cooked unevenly, or not enough water.
items.fix: The lentils need slow, sustained heat to melt into the sauce. Add more water if the base looks dry and reduce the heat further.

items.cause: Cut too large, or heat too low.
items.fix: Lamb shoulder at 4 cm cubes needs 60–75 minutes of gentle simmering. Give it another 20 minutes covered.

items.cause: Tamarind added at the start rather than the end.
items.fix: Add tamarind at the very end of cooking, after the sauce has thickened. Added early, it becomes muted. Taste and adjust with more tamarind or a pinch of sugar.

content.text: Dhansak is Parsi community food — made for Parsi new year, for Sundays, and in the British-Indian restaurant context where it became associated with a particular style of hot-sweet-sour curry. The Parsi community, descendants of Zoroastrian Persians who settled in Gujarat around the 10th century, developed a cooking style that fuses Persian and Gujarati elements. Dhansak shows this clearly: the technique of braising meat with lentils is Persian; the spicing is Gujarati; the tamarind sourness is South Indian. The British-Indian restaurant version simplified the dish somewhat, but kept its essential character intact.
